lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: There is no composition in these news

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: That gives them credit.

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Indeed, they are disproportion’d;

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: My letters say a hundred and seven galleys.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: And mine, a hundred and forty.

lines.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
lines.text: And mine, two hundred:

lines.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
lines.text: But though they jump not on a just account—

lines.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
lines.text: As in these cases, where the aim reports,

lines.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
lines.text: ’Tis oft with difference—yet do they all confirm

lines.speaker: SECOND SENATOR
lines.text: A Turkish fleet, and bearing up to Cyprus.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Nay, it is possible enough to judgment:

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: I do not so secure me in the error,

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: But the main article I do approve

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: In fearful sense.

lines.speaker: SAILOR
lines.text: What, ho! what, ho! what, ho!

lines.speaker: FIRST OFFICER
lines.text: A messenger from the galleys.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Now, what’s the business?

lines.speaker: SAILOR
lines.text: The Turkish preparation makes for Rhodes;

lines.speaker: SAILOR
lines.text: So was I bid report here to the state

lines.speaker: SAILOR
lines.text: By Signior Angelo.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: How say you by this change?

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: This cannot be,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: By no assay of reason: ’tis a pageant,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: To keep us in false gaze. When we consider

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: The importancy of Cyprus to the Turk,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: And let ourselves again but understand,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: That as it more concerns the Turk than Rhodes,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: So may he with more facile question bear it,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: For that it stands not in such warlike brace,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: But altogether lacks the abilities

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: That Rhodes is dress’d in: if we make thought of this,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: We must not think the Turk is so unskilful

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: To leave that latest which concerns him first,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Neglecting an attempt of ease and gain,

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: To wake and wage a danger profitless.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Nay, in all confidence, he’s not for Rhodes.

lines.speaker: FIRST OFFICER
lines.text: Here is more news.

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: The Ottomites, reverend and gracious,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Steering with due course towards the isle of Rhodes,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Have there injointed them with an after fleet.

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Ay, so I thought. How many, as you guess?

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Of thirty sail: and now they do restem

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Their backward course, bearing with frank appearance

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Their purposes toward Cyprus. Signior Montano,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: Your trusty and most valiant servitor,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: With his free duty recommends you thus,

lines.speaker: MESSENGER
lines.text: And prays you to believe him.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: ’Tis certain, then, for Cyprus.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Marcus Luccicos, is not he in town?

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: He’s now in Florence.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Write from us to him; post-post-haste dispatch.

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Here comes Brabantio and the valiant Moor.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Valiant Othello, we must straight employ you

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Against the general enemy Ottoman.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: To Brabantio. I did not see you; welcome, gentle signior;

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: We lack’d your counsel and your help tonight.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: So did I yours. Good your grace, pardon me;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Neither my place nor aught I heard of business

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Hath raised me from my bed, nor doth the general care

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Take hold on me, for my particular grief

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Is of so flood-gate and o’erbearing nature

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: That it engluts and swallows other sorrows

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: And it is still itself.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Why, what’s the matter?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: My daughter! O, my daughter!

lines.speaker: DUKE SENATOR
lines.text: Dead?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Ay, to me;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: She is abused, stol’n from me, and corrupted

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: By spells and medicines bought of mountebanks;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: For nature so preposterously to err,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Being not deficient, blind, or lame of sense,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Sans witchcraft could not.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Whoe’er he be that in this foul proceeding

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Hath thus beguiled your daughter of herself

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: And you of her, the bloody book of law

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: You shall yourself read in the bitter letter

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: After your own sense, yea, though our proper son

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Stood in your action.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Humbly I thank your grace.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Here is the man, this Moor, whom now, it seems,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Your special mandate for the state-affairs

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Hath hither brought.

lines.speaker: DUKE SENATOR
lines.text: We are very sorry for’t.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: To Othello. What, in your own part, can you say to this?

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Nothing, but this is so.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Most potent, grave, and reverend signiors,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My very noble and approved good masters,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That I have ta’en away this old man’s daughter,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It is most true; true, I have married her:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The very head and front of my offending

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Hath this extent, no more. Rude am I in my speech,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And little bless’d with the soft phrase of peace:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For since these arms of mine had seven years’ pith,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Till now some nine moons wasted, they have used

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Their dearest action in the tented field,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And little of this great world can I speak,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: More than pertains to feats of broil and battle,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And therefore little shall I grace my cause

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In speaking for myself. Yet, by your gracious patience,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I will a round unvarnish’d tale deliver

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of my whole course of love; what drugs, what charms,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: What conjuration and what mighty magic,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: For such proceeding I am charged withal,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I won his daughter.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: A maiden never bold;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Of spirit so still and quiet, that her motion

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Blush’d at herself; and she, in spite of nature,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Of years, of country, credit, every thing,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: To fall in love with what she fear’d to look on!

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: It is a judgment maim’d and most imperfect

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: That will confess perfection so could err

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Against all rules of nature, and must be driven

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: To find out practises of cunning hell,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Why this should be. I therefore vouch again

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: That with some mixtures powerful o’er the blood,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Or with some dram conjured to this effect,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: He wrought upon her.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: To vouch this, is no proof,

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Without more wider and more overt test

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Than these thin habits and poor likelihoods

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Of modern seeming do prefer against him.

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: But, Othello, speak:

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Did you by indirect and forced courses

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Subdue and poison this young maid’s affections?

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Or came it by request and such fair question

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: As soul to soul affordeth?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I do beseech you,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Send for the lady to the Sagittary,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And let her speak of me before her father:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If you do find me foul in her report,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The trust, the office I do hold of you,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Not only take away, but let your sentence

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Even fall upon my life.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Fetch Desdemona hither.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Ancient, conduct them: you best know the place.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And, till she come, as truly as to heaven

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I do confess the vices of my blood,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: So justly to your grave ears I’ll present

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How I did thrive in this fair lady’s love,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And she in mine.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Say it, Othello.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Her father loved me; oft invited me;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Still question’d me the story of my life,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: From year to year, the battles, sieges, fortunes,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That I have passed.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I ran it through, even from my boyish days,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To the very moment that he bade me tell it;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Wherein I spake of most disastrous chances,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of moving accidents by flood and field

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of hair-breadth scapes i’ the imminent deadly breach,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of being taken by the insolent foe

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And sold to slavery, of my redemption thence

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And portance in my travels’ history:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Wherein of antres vast and deserts idle,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Rough quarries, rocks and hills whose heads touch heaven

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It was my hint to speak—such was the process;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And of the Cannibals that each other eat,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The Anthropophagi and men whose heads

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Do grow beneath their shoulders. This to hear

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Would Desdemona seriously incline:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But still the house-affairs would draw her thence:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Which ever as she could with haste dispatch,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She’d come again, and with a greedy ear

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Devour up my discourse: which I observing,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Took once a pliant hour, and found good means

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To draw from her a prayer of earnest heart

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That I would all my pilgrimage dilate,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Whereof by parcels she had something heard,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: But not intentively: I did consent,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And often did beguile her of her tears,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: When I did speak of some distressful stroke

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That my youth suffer’d. My story being done,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She gave me for my pains a world of sighs:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She swore, in faith, ’twas strange, ’twas passing strange,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Twas pitiful, ’twas wondrous pitiful:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She wish’d she had not heard it, yet she wish’d

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That heaven had made her such a man: she thank’d me,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And bade me, if I had a friend that loved her,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I should but teach him how to tell my story.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And that would woo her. Upon this hint I spake:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: She loved me for the dangers I had pass’d,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And I loved her that she did pity them.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: This only is the witchcraft I have used:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Here comes the lady; let her witness it.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: I think this tale would win my daughter too.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Good Brabantio,

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Take up this mangled matter at the best:

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Men do their broken weapons rather use

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Than their bare hands.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I pray you, hear her speak:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: If she confess that she was half the wooer,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Destruction on my head, if my bad blame

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Light on the man! Come hither, gentle mistress:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Do you perceive in all this noble company

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Where most you owe obedience?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My noble father,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I do perceive here a divided duty:

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To you I am bound for life and education;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My life and education both do learn me

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: How to respect you; you are the lord of duty;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I am hitherto your daughter: but here’s my husband,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And so much duty as my mother show’d

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To you, preferring you before her father,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: So much I challenge that I may profess

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Due to the Moor my lord.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: God be wi’ you! I have done.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Please it your grace, on to the state-affairs:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I had rather to adopt a child than get it.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Come hither, Moor:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I here do give thee that with all my heart

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Which, but thou hast already, with all my heart

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I would keep from thee. For your sake, jewel,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I am glad at soul I have no other child:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: For thy escape would teach me tyranny,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: To hang clogs on them. I have done, my lord.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Let me speak like yourself, and lay a sentence,

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Which, as a grise or step, may help these lovers

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Into your favour.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: When remedies are past, the griefs are ended

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: By seeing the worst, which late on hopes depended.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: To mourn a mischief that is past and gone

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Is the next way to draw new mischief on.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: What cannot be preserved when fortune takes

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Patience her injury a mockery makes.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: The robb’d that smiles steals something from the thief;

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: He robs himself that spends a bootless grief.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: So let the Turk of Cyprus us beguile;

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: We lose it not, so long as we can smile.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: He bears the sentence well that nothing bears

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: But the free comfort which from thence he hears,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: But he bears both the sentence and the sorrow

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: That, to pay grief, must of poor patience borrow.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: These sentences, to sugar, or to gall,

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Being strong on both sides, are equivocal:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: But words are words; I never yet did hear

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: That the bruised heart was pierced through the ear.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: I humbly beseech you, proceed to the affairs of state.

lines.text: The Turk with a most mighty preparation makes for Cyprus. Othello, the

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: fortitude of the place is best known to you; and though we

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: have there a substitute of most allowed sufficiency, yet opinion, a sovereign

lines.text: mistress of effects, throws a more safer voice on you: you must

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: therefore be content to slubber the gloss of your new fortunes with

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: this more stubborn and boisterous expedition.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: The tyrant custom, most grave senators,

lines.text: Hath made the flinty and steel couch of war

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My thrice-driven bed of down: I do agnise

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: A natural and prompt alacrity

lines.text: I find in hardness, and do undertake

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: These present wars against the Ottomites.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Most humbly therefore bending to your state,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I crave fit disposition for my wife.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Due reference of place and exhibition,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: With such accommodation and besort

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As levels with her breeding.

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: If you please,

lines.speaker: DUKE

lines.text: I’ll not have it so.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Nor I.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Nor I; I would not there reside,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To put my father in impatient thoughts

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: By being in his eye. Most gracious duke,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To my unfolding lend your prosperous ear;

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: And let me find a charter in your voice,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To assist my simpleness.

lines.text: What would you, Desdemona?

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: That I did love the Moor to live with him,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My downright violence and storm of fortunes

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: May trumpet to the world: my heart’s subdued

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I saw Othello’s visage in his mind,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: So that, dear lords, if I be left behind,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: A moth of peace, and he go to the war,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: The rites for which I love him are bereft me,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: By his dear absence. Let me go with him.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Let her have your voices.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Vouch with me, heaven, I therefore beg it not,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To please the palate of my appetite,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO

lines.text: Of feather’d Cupid seal with wanton dullness

lines.speaker: OTHELLO

lines.text: And speed must answer it.

lines.text: You must away to-night.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: With all my heart.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: With what else needful your good grace shall think

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: And, noble signior,

lines.text: If virtue no delighted beauty lack,

lines.speaker: DUKE
lines.text: Your son-in-law is far more fair than black.

lines.speaker: FIRST SENATOR
lines.text: Adieu, brave Moor, use Desdemona well.

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: Look to her, Moor, if thou hast eyes to see:

lines.speaker: BRABANTIO
lines.text: She has deceived her father, and may thee.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My life upon her faith! Honest Iago,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My Desdemona must I leave to thee:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I prithee, let thy wife attend on her:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And bring them after in the best advantage.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Come, Desdemona: I have but an hour

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Of love, of worldly matters and direction,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To spend with thee: we must obey the time.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Iago—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: What say’st thou, noble heart?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: What will I do, thinkest thou?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Why, go to bed, and sleep.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I will incontinently drown myself.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If thou dost, I shall never love thee after. Why, thou silly gentleman!

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: It is silliness to live when to live is torment; and then

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: have we a prescription to die when death is our physician.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O villainous! I have looked upon the world for four times seven

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: years; and since I could distinguish betwixt a benefit and an injury,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I never found man that knew how to love himself. Ere I

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: would say, I would drown myself for the love of a guinea-hen,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I would change my humanity with a baboon.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: What should I do? I confess it is my shame to be

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: so fond; but it is not in my virtue to amend it.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Virtue! a fig! ’tis in ourselves that we are thus or thus.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Our bodies are our gardens, to the which our wills are gardeners:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: so that if we will plant nettles, or sow lettuce, set hyssop

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and weed up thyme, supply it with one gender of herbs, or

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: distract it with many, either to have it sterile with idleness, or

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: manured with industry, why, the power and corrigible authority of this lies

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: in our wills. If the balance of our lives had not one

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: scale of reason to poise another of sensuality, the blood and baseness

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: of our natures would conduct us to most preposterous conclusions: but we

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: have reason to cool our raging motions, our carnal stings, our unbitted

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: lusts, whereof I take this that you call love to be a

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: sect or scion.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: It cannot be.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It is merely a lust of the blood and a permission of

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the will. Come, be a man. Drown thyself! drown cats and blind

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: puppies. I have professed me thy friend and I confess me knit

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: to thy deserving with cables of perdurable toughness; I could never better

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: stead thee than now. Put money in thy purse; follow thou the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: wars; defeat thy favour with an usurped beard; I say, put money

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: in thy purse. It cannot be that Desdemona should long continue her

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: love to the Moor—put money in thy purse—nor he his to her:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: it was a violent commencement, and thou shalt see an answerable sequestration:—put

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: but money in thy purse. These Moors are changeable in their wills:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: fill thy purse with money:—the food that to him now is as

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: luscious as locusts, shall be to him shortly as bitter as coloquintida.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She must change for youth: when she is sated with his body,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: she will find the error of her choice: she must have change,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: she must: therefore put money in thy purse. If thou wilt needs

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: damn thyself, do it a more delicate way than drowning. Make all

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the money thou canst: if sanctimony and a frail vow betwixt an

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: erring barbarian and a supersubtle Venetian not too hard for my wits

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and all the tribe of hell, thou shalt enjoy her; therefore make

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: money. A pox of drowning thyself! it is clean out of the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: way: seek thou rather to be hanged in compassing thy joy than

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: to be drowned and go without her.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Wilt thou be fast to my hopes, if I depend on the issue?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Thou art sure of me:—go, make money:—I have told thee often, and

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I re-tell thee again and again, I hate the Moor: my cause

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: is hearted; thine hath no less reason. Let us be conjunctive in

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: our revenge against him: if thou canst cuckold him, thou dost thyself

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: a pleasure, me a sport. There are many events in the womb

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: of time which will be delivered. Traverse! go, provide thy money. We

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: will have more of this to-morrow. Adieu.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Where shall we meet i’ the morning?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: At my lodging.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I’ll be with thee betimes.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Go to; farewell. Do you hear, Roderigo?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: What say you?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: No more of drowning, do you hear?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I am changed: I’ll go sell all my land.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Thus do I ever make my fool my purse:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For I mine own gain’d knowledge should profane,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If I would time expend with such a snipe.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But for my sport and profit. I hate the Moor:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And it is thought abroad, that ’twixt my sheets

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He has done my office: I know not if’t be true;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But I, for mere suspicion in that kind,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Will do as if for surety. He holds me well;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The better shall my purpose work on him.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Cassio’s a proper man: let me see now:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To get his place and to plume up my will

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In double knavery—How, how? Let’s see:—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: After some time, to abuse Othello’s ear

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That he is too familiar with his wife.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He hath a person and a smooth dispose

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To be suspected, framed to make women false.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The Moor is of a free and open nature,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That thinks men honest that but seem to be so,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And will as tenderly be led by the nose

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As asses are.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I have’t. It is engender’d. Hell and night

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Must bring this monstrous birth to the world’s light.

stage_directions.text: The Duke and Senators sitting at a table; Officers attending.
Enter Brabantio, Othello, Iago, Roderigo, and Officers.

glosses.phrase: with more facile question bear it

glosses.definition: Ottoman Turks — the naval threat to Venice's empire
honored and gracious — formal address to the senators
turn back against; sail back toward

glosses.phrase: Rude am I in my speech
glosses.definition: my speech is rough, unpolished — Othello's humility formula before his great address
at seven years old; since I was seven
stirring incidents; events that move the heart
narrow gap in a wall where death was a heartbeat away
cannibals — from Greek 'man-eaters'; a legendary race reported by Pliny and Mandeville
a propitious moment; a favorable opening

glosses.phrase: Take up this mangled matter at the best
glosses.definition: make the best you can of this damaged situation
step; a level on a stair
maxim; aphorism — Brabantio mocks the Duke's platitudes
sweetness; words that sweeten a bitter fact
tarnish the brightness; soil the newly polished
the soldier's stony bed — the battlefield ground
a feather mattress with down triple-sorted for softness

glosses.phrase: downright violence and storm of fortunes
glosses.definition: my open breach with custom; the headlong boldness of my love
a cant term for a prostitute; a kept woman
cutting or offshoot of a plant — here, an offspring of passion
sweet carob pods — named in Matthew 3:4 as John the Baptist's food
a long-beaked marsh bird; proverbially a gull or fool

annotations.title: Rude am I in my speech: the Senate speech as autobiography
annotations.body:
Othello's defence before the Venetian senate is the most extended self-representation of any Shakespearean hero. The claim 'rude am I in my speech' is, of course, a rhetorical figure — *occupatio*, the pretence of plainness — followed by a speech of exceptional rhythmical assurance. The content is autobiography: bondage, battle, travel, cannibals and the Blemmyae, the household where Brabantio invited him. Love grew from narration. Desdemona, he insists, 'loved me for the dangers I had passed, / And I loved her that she did pity them.'

Critical tradition divides here. T. S. Eliot heard the speech as Othello 'cheering himself up', the man already composing his own myth. F. R. Leavis extended the charge: Othello's tragedy begins not in Iago's malice but in this unearned self-idealization. A. C. Bradley took the speech at face value: a noble soul speaking noble truths.

Read either way, the speech establishes the currency Iago will debase. Othello's authority rests on the coherence of his story. By 3.3 he will have lost the ability to tell it.
sources: T. S. Eliot, 'Shakespeare and the Stoicism of Seneca' (1927)
F. R. Leavis, 'Diabolic Intellect and the Noble Hero', *Scrutiny* (1937)

annotations.title: The Anthropophagi and Renaissance travel literature
annotations.body:
The cannibals and headless men 'whose heads do grow beneath their shoulders' are not Shakespeare's invention. They come from Pliny's *Natural History* through Mandeville's *Travels* and Raleigh's *Discoverie of Guiana* (1596). The Blemmyae — headless humans with faces in their chests — were standard furniture of the early-modern imagination of Africa and the New World.

The audience would have recognized the catalogue and registered it two ways at once: as marvelous travel data (still credited) and as a literary commonplace. Desdemona's response — she 'seriously inclined' to hear it — shows her treating wonder literature as ethical knowledge rather than entertainment. Shakespeare is quietly noting what kind of reader she is.
sources: Margaret T. Hodgen, *Early Anthropology in the Sixteenth and Seventeenth Centuries* (1964)
Peter Mason, *Deconstructing America: Representations of the Other* (1990)

annotations.title: Iago's first soliloquy: the plan is not yet the plan
annotations.body:
Notice what Iago does *not* yet have. He has a grievance over the lieutenancy, a purse to milk from Roderigo, and a shaped suspicion — 'it is thought abroad that 'twixt my sheets / He's done my office.' He does not have a plan. What he has is a method: 'After some time, to abuse Othello's ear.'

The soliloquy's most infamous line — 'I hate the Moor' — is flanked on both sides by speculation, not conviction. The motive is multiple and shifting: professional resentment, sexual suspicion, a general love of mischief. This is the 'motiveless malignity' Coleridge named — not the absence of motive but motive as improvisation. Iago will know what he wants only as the scenes produce him it.

The shape of the soliloquy matters for the structure of the play: Iago's evil is a compositional method, not a predetermined design. He will improvise at every step, and the tragedy will unfold scene by scene as his opportunities unfold.
sources: William Empson, *The Structure of Complex Words* (1951), ch. 11 'Honest in Othello'
Stanley Cavell, *Disowning Knowledge in Seven Plays of Shakespeare* (rev. 2003)

trials.prompt: When the senators debate the Turkish fleet's destination — Rhodes or Cyprus — what conclusion do they reach?
options: Rhodes is the real target; Cyprus is a feint
Cyprus is the real target; Rhodes is a feint to draw Venetian forces away
Both islands will be attacked simultaneously
The reports are contradictory and no action can be taken

trials.prompt: In his long speech beginning 'Rude am I in my speech,' Othello claims his rhetorical gifts are limited. How should the audience read this claim?
options: As a simple truthful self-description
As false modesty — a rhetorical figure (occupatio) opening an exceptionally well-made speech
As evidence that Othello secretly has speech impediments
As Shakespeare apologizing for an earlier draft of the scene

trials.prompt: According to Othello, how did Desdemona come to love him?
options: Her father arranged the match for political advantage
Through repeated social visits she gradually came to know him
She loved him for the dangerous stories he told, and he loved her for pitying those dangers
She received love letters written by Cassio on his behalf

trials.prompt: Othello describes meeting 'Anthropophagi, and men whose heads do grow beneath their shoulders.' What are these?
options: Shakespeare's invented creatures
Classical legends of cannibals and headless humans (Blemmyae) from Pliny and Mandeville
Nicknames for particular Ottoman tribes
Characters from earlier Italian plays

trials.prompt: In his closing soliloquy, Iago articulates multiple possible reasons to hate Othello. What does this multiplicity suggest about his motivation?
options: He is logically ranking the most important reasons for revenge
His motives are improvised and shifting — Coleridge called it 'motiveless malignity'
He is lying to himself to hide a single real reason
The soliloquy was added later and reflects the views of a collaborator

trials.prompt: What does the Duke mean by telling Brabantio that he should 'take the robb'd that smiles' and steal something from the thief?
options: Brabantio should literally repay the theft by stealing from Othello
Accepting fate with good humor is better than ongoing grief; Brabantio should move on
It is a Venetian proverb about contract law
Brabantio should accuse Othello of embezzlement

trials.prompt: What state assignment does the Duke give to Othello at the end of the council?
options: Remain in Venice as chief military advisor
Lead the defence of Cyprus against the Turks
Negotiate a peace treaty with Istanbul
Retire from service and reconcile with Brabantio
